Illish pronouns use many English neopronouns

Over time the pronoun system became more regularized due to the presence of neopronouns

This includes 

* the eventual development of an -m suffix on all object forms
** Started with the neopronouns
** Moved to Me becoming Meme since it is reduplication
** Then Youm
** Then finally Us became Um after pronounciation became too similar to Ours
* all dependent possessives end with -ra (a stronger enunciation of the -r after it started to get challenged by tonogenesis, similar things happened on rising tone final syllable words and on the various grammatical suffixes that later became the tone Suprafixes.)
*

{| class="wikitable"
!
! colspan="2" |Subject
! colspan="2" |Object
! colspan="2" |Dependent pos
! colspan="2" |Independent pos
! colspan="2" |Reflexive
|-
!
! English
! Illish 
!English!! Illish 
!English!! Illish 
!English!! Illish 
!English!! Illish
|-
| Informal 1st person
| I
| Aī 
|Me|| Mīm 
|My|| Maīra 
|Mine|| Mãīs 
|Myself|| Maīseēf
|-
| Formal 1st person
| We
| Wī 
|Us|| Ām 
|Our|| Arā 
|Ours|| Aās 
|Ourself|| Aāseēf
|-
| 2nd person
| You
| Yū 
|You|| Yūm 
|Your|| Yoōrā 
|Yours|| Yoōs 
|Yourself|| Yoōseēf
|-
| 3rd person (animate)
| They
| Deī 
|Them|| Dēm 
|Their|| Deērā 
|Theirs|| Deēs 
|Theirself|| Deēseēf
|-
| Inanimate
| It
| I 
|It|| I 
|Its|| I 
|Its|| I 
|Itself|| Iseēf
|-
| 4th person
| Fae
| Feī 
|Faem|| Fēm 
|Faer|| Feērā 
|Faers|| Feēs 
|Faerself|| Feēseēf
|-
|5th person
|Xe
|Zeī
|Xem
|Zēm
|Xeir
|Zeērā
|Xeirs
|Zeēs
|Xeirself
|Zeēseēf
|-
| 6th person
| She
| Sheī 
|Her|| Shem 
|Her|| Sheērā 
|Hers|| Sheēs 
|Herself|| Sheēseēf
|-
| 7th person
| He
| Heī 
|Him|| Hem 
|His|| Heērā 
|His|| Heēs 
|Himself|| Heēseēf
|-
| 8th person
| Vi
| Veī 
|Vim|| Vēm 
|Vir|| Veērā 
|Virs|| Veēs 
|Virself|| Veēseēf
|-
| 9th person
| Ne
| Neī 
|Nem|| Nēm 
|Neir|| Neērā 
|Neirs|| Neēs 
|Neirself|| Neēseēf
|-
| 10th person
| Per
| Peī 
|Per|| Pēm 
|Per|| Peērā 
|Pers|| Peēs 
|Perself|| Peēseēf
|}

== Change timeline ==
We are only including the singular tone for this purpose

=== I ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom (Subject)
! Acc (Object)
! Dat (Indirect Obj)
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| *éǵh₂
| *mé
| *moi
| (genitive forms only)<ref>PIE did not have dependent possessives; possession was expressed by genitive case.</ref>
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| All cases distinct
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *ik
| *mik
| *miz
| (from *meina)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| 4-case system; all distinct
|-
| Old English (450–1100)
| '''ic'''
| '''me'''
| '''me'''
| '''mīn'''
| '''mīn'''
| ''ic self''<ref>“self” was still a fully inflected noun in OE and not yet part of a reflexive pronoun.</ref>
| First major merger: acc/dat → "me"
|-
| Middle English (1100–1500)
| '''I'''
| colspan="2" | '''me'''
| '''my'''
| '''mine'''<ref>Independent possessive ("mine") arises from phonological full-vs-reduced alternation.</ref>
| '''myself'''<ref>Reflexives originate from pronoun + “self,” which was reanalyzed as a bound morpheme.</ref>
| Possessive splits by analogy → my/mine
|-
| Modern English
| '''I'''
| colspan="2" | '''me'''
| '''my'''
| '''mine'''
| '''myself'''
| Reflexive created analogically
|-
|Illish
|Aī
| colspan="2" |Mīm<ref>-m object form came back in analogy with neopronouns and reduplication</ref>
|Maī
|Maīn
|Maīseēf
|Became informal 1st person after tones started to mark number
|}

=== We ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom (Subject)
! Acc (Object)
! Dat
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| *wéy
| *ns-me
| *ns-moi
| *ns-osyo (genitive)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| PIE used case endings, not separate possessives
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *wīz
| *uns
| *uns
| *unsara
| (same)
| Reflexive only for 3rd person (*sik*)
| 4-case system; acc/dat already converging
|-
| Old English (450–1100)
| '''wē'''
| '''ūs'''
| '''ūs'''
| '''ūre'''
| '''ūre'''
| ''wē selfe''<ref>OE “self” was still an independent noun, not a bound reflexive suffix.</ref>
| Acc/dat fully merged into “ūs”
|-
| Middle English (1100–1500)
| '''we'''
| '''us'''
| '''us'''
| '''our'''
| '''ours'''<ref>Independent possessive arises by same analogy as my/mine, thy/thine, their/theirs.</ref>
| '''ourselves'''<ref>Reflexive created from pronoun + “selves” by Middle English analogy.</ref>
| Possessive splits by analogy → our / ours
|-
| Modern English
| '''we'''
| colspan="2" | '''us'''
| '''our'''
| '''ours'''
| ourselves
| Stable plural paradigm; reflexive fully grammaticalized
|-
|Illish
|Wī
| colspan="2" |Ām<ref>Because Aās and As were too close ended up taking -m by analogy. Last of the pronouns to do that</ref>
|Aā
|Aās
|Aāseēf
|Became formal 1st person after tones started to mark number
|}

=== You ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom (Subject)
! Acc (Object)
! Dat
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| *túh₂
| *té
| *toi
| (genitive forms only)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| All distinct
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *þū
| *þek
| *þiz
| *þeina
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| Distinct forms
|-
| Old English (singular)
| '''þū'''
| '''þē'''
| '''þē'''
| '''þīn'''
| '''þīn'''
| ''þū self''
| Singular kept distinctions
|-
| Old English (plural)
| '''gē'''
| '''ēow'''
| '''ēow'''
| '''ēower'''
| '''ēower'''
| ''gē selfe''
| Plural also distinct
|-
| Middle English
| thou / ye
| thee / you
| thee / you
| thy / your
| thine / yours
| thyself / yourself
| Start of mergers; "you" rises as polite singular
|-
| Early Modern
| '''you'''
| colspan="2" | '''you'''
| '''your'''
| '''yours'''
| yourself / yourselves
| SUBJECT/OBJECT merge fully
|-
| Modern English
| '''you'''
| colspan="2" | '''you'''
| '''your'''
| '''yours'''
| yourself / yourselves
| Most collapsed pronoun paradigm
|-
|
|Yū
| colspan="2" |Yūm
|Yoō
|Yoōs
|Yoōseēf
|
|-
|Illish
|ju
| colspan="2" |jɛm<ref>Object came back in analogy with neopronouns and Me/Meme</ref>
|jo'''ː'''
|jo'''ːs'''
|jo'''ː'''sɛ˥f
|
|}
=== He ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom
! Acc
! Dat
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| *h₁éy / *so
| *(h₁)eṃ
| *éyemi
| *ésyo
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| No true 3rd-person; demonstratives used
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *iz
| *iną
| *im
| *is
| (same)
| Reflexive *sik*
| Full case system; acc/dat distinct
|-
| Old English
| '''hē'''
| '''hine'''
| '''him'''
| '''his'''
| '''his'''
| ''hē self''
| Acc & dat distinct
|-
| Late Old English
| '''hē'''
| ('''him''' replaces hine)
| '''him'''
| '''his'''
| '''his'''
| ''him self''
| Acc → dat merger begins
|-
| Middle English
| '''he'''
| '''him'''
| '''him'''
| '''his'''
| '''his'''
| '''himself'''
| Merger complete; no new analogies
|-
| Modern English
| '''he'''
| colspan="2" | '''him'''
| '''his'''
| '''his'''
| himself
| Stable paradigm
|-
|
|He
| colspan="2" |Him
|Hir
|Hir
|Hirself
|
|-
|Illish
|Heī
| colspan="2" |Hēm
|Heē
|Heēs
|Heēseēf
|Reduced to marginal usage and pronounciation taken from they
|}
=== Her ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom
! Acc
! Dat
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| PIE had no 3rd-person personal pronoun
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| (demonstrative forms)
| (demonstrative forms)
| (demonstrative forms)
| (demonstrative genitives)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| Feminine derived from demonstratives
|-
| Old English
| '''heo'''
| '''hīe'''
| '''hire'''
| '''hire'''
| '''hire'''
| ''hēo self''
| Acc = hīe; dat/gen = hire
|-
| Middle English
| '''she'''<ref>Origin likely from Anglian "scho" or reanalysis of OE "seo".</ref>
| '''her'''
| '''her'''
| '''her'''
| '''hers'''<ref>Independent possessive created by analogy with mine/thine.</ref>
| '''herself'''
| Massive mergers: acc/dat/gen → "her"
|-
| Modern English
| '''she'''
| colspan="2" | '''her'''
| '''her'''
| '''hers'''
| herself
| Most collapsed 3rd-person system
|-
|She line
|She
| colspan="2" |Shem
|Sher
|Shers
|Shemself/Herself
|
|-
|Her line
|Her
| colspan="2" |Herm
|Her
|Hers
|Herself
|
|-
|Illish
|Sheī
| colspan="2" |Shēm<ref name=":0">Pronounciation taken from they/them with just the sh- initial kept

However many consider this the successor pronoun. The same [[kanji]] is used across both.</ref>
|Sheē<ref name=":0" />
|Sheēs<ref name=":0" />
|Sheēseēf<ref name=":0" />
|Reduced to marginal usage and pronounciation taken from they
|}
===They===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom (Subject)
! Acc (Object)
! Dat
! Dependent pos
! Independent pos
! Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (demonstratives)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| PIE had no personal pronoun for 3rd-plural
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *þai
| *þanz
| *þaim
| *þaizō
| (same)
| Reflexive *sik*
| Distinct paradigms
|-
| Old English (native)
| '''hīe'''
| '''hīe'''
| '''him'''
| '''heora'''
| '''heora'''
| ''hīe selfe''
| Native forms (not ancestors of modern “they”)
|-
| Old Norse (borrowed set)
| '''þeir'''
| '''þá'''
| '''þeim'''
| '''þeira'''
| '''þeira'''
| ''þeir sjálfr''
| Distinct 4-case paradigm; borrowed in Danelaw
|-
| Middle English (early)
| they
| them
| them (dat merged)
| their
| theirs
| themselves<ref>“selves” ending created by analogy with our/your + selves.</ref>
| Norse forms replace English ones
|-
| Middle English (late)
| '''they'''
| colspan="2" | '''them'''
| '''their'''
| '''theirs'''
| themselves
| Possessive splits by analogy
|-
| Modern English
| '''they'''
| colspan="2" | '''them'''
| '''their'''
| '''theirs'''
| themselves / themself<ref>“Themself” appears in the 1300s; revived for singular they.</ref>
| Stable paradigm; also used as singular
|-
|Illish romanization
|Deī
| colspan="2" |Dēm
|Deē
|Deēs
|Deēseēf
|
|-
|Illish
|ðeɪ
| colspan="2" |dɛm
|ðɛɚ
|ðɛɚz
|ðɛɚsɛ˥f
|
|}
=== It ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Stage
! Nom
! Acc
! Dat
! Dependent pos
!Independent pos
!Reflexive
! Notes
|-
| Proto-Indo-European
| (inanimate demonstratives)
| (inanimate demonstratives)
| (inanimate datives)
| (genitive demonstratives)
| (n/a)
| (n/a)
| Inanimates did not use reflexives
|-
| Proto-Germanic
| *þat
| *þat
| *þata
| *þas
| (same)
| (n/a)
| All demonstrative-based
|-
| Old English
| '''hit'''
| '''hit'''
| '''him'''
| '''his'''
| '''his'''
| ''hit self''
| Nominative = accusative
|-
| Middle English
| '''it'''
| '''it'''
| '''it'''
| '''its'''<ref>“its” emerges in 17th century; before that, “his” used for neuter possessive.</ref>
| '''its'''
| itself
| Dat merges; "its" appears in 1600s
|-
| Modern English
| '''it'''
| '''it'''
| (merged)
| '''its'''
| '''its'''
| itself
| Total collapse except possessive
|-
|Illish
|ɪ˩
|ɪ˩
|ɪ˩
|ɪ˩
|ɪ˩
|ɪ˩sɛ˥f
|Tone removed coda and genitives lost
|}


== References ==

[[Category:Illish]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
